steps.title: Condition for infinitely many solutions
steps.content: A system of two linear equations has infinitely many solutions when the two equations represent the same line — i.e., one equation is a constant multiple of the other.

steps.title: Test each system
steps.content:
Check whether equation 2 = k × equation 1 for some constant k.

Choice A: \(4x+2y=12\) and \(4x+8y=48\). Ratio of x-coefficients: 4/4 = 1. Ratio of y-coefficients: 8/2 = 4. Not equal — not the same line.

Choice B: \(x+2y=12\) and \(x+8y=48\). Ratio of x: 1/1 = 1. Ratio of y: 8/2 = 4. Not equal — not the same line.

Choice C: \(x+2y=12\) and \(4x+8y=48\). Multiply the first equation by 4: \(4x+8y=48\). This is identical to the second equation. Same line! Infinitely many solutions.

Choice D: \(4x+2y=12\) and \(x+8y=48\). Ratio of x: 1/4. Ratio of y: 8/2 = 4. Not equal — not the same line.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — '\(4x+2y=12\) and \(4x+8y=48\)': Different slopes; two distinct intersecting lines (exactly one solution). ✗
  • Choice B — '\(x+2y=12\) and \(x+8y=48\)': Different slopes; two distinct intersecting lines. ✗
  • Choice C — '\(x+2y=12\) and \(4x+8y=48\)': Second equation is 4 × first; identical lines, infinitely many solutions. ✓
  • Choice D — '\(4x+2y=12\) and \(x+8y=48\)': Not proportional; two distinct intersecting lines. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
